primerTitle: C++ control flow & functions — quick recap
primer:
## Control flow
**Loops:** `for` (known count / explicit counter), `while` (tests before the body, may run zero times), `do { ... } while (cond);` (runs once, then tests), and the **range-based for** `for (auto x : v)` which iterates a container/array without an index — `for (auto& x : v)` to modify in place, `for (const auto& x : v)` to read large elements without copying.

In a `switch`, a `case` **falls through** into the next unless you write `break` (or `return`) — a classic bug source. `break` exits the nearest loop/switch entirely; `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and re-tests the condition. The **ternary** `cond ? x : y` is an expression form of if/else (`int m = (a > b) ? a : b;`), so it can sit inside an initialiser or argument.

**Scope** is the region where a name is visible. A variable in `{ ... }` is local to that block and destroyed at the closing brace; inner scopes can shadow outer names. File scope = global.

## Functions
A **declaration** (prototype) states name, return type, parameter types and ends with `;` (`int add(int, int);`); a **definition** adds the body `{ ... }`. Declare many times, define exactly once (One Definition Rule).

**Pass-by-value** copies the argument (use for small cheap types or a private copy); **pass-by-reference** `T&` aliases the caller's object so changes are visible. Pass a large object as **`const T&`**: the reference avoids a copy and `const` guarantees read-only — the default for non-cheap read-only params.

A **default argument** supplies a value when the caller omits it (`int pow(int b, int e = 2);`); defaults must be trailing and given once in the declaration. **Overloading** = several functions with the same name but different parameter lists, resolved by argument types (return type alone cannot distinguish them). A `void` function returns no value (bare `return;` exits early). **Recursion** is a function calling itself; it needs a base case to stop and a step toward it (`factorial(0)=1`; `factorial(n)=n*factorial(n-1)`) — a missing base case overflows the stack.

## Pitfalls
Returning a reference/pointer to a **local** is a bug: the local dies when the function returns, leaving a dangling reference (UB) — return by value instead. An `unsigned` can never be negative, so `for (unsigned i = 5; i >= 0; --i)` loops forever (`--i` at 0 wraps to the max value).
name: C++ — Control flow & functions
description: Decision and loop constructs, then functions: declaration vs definition, parameters by value vs by reference, default arguments, overloading and scope. Builds directly on the syntax & types deck.

cards.term: What are the loop constructs in C++?
cards.definition: `for` (when you know the count or want an explicit counter), `while` (test before the body, may run zero times), and `do { ... } while (cond);` (runs the body at least once, then tests). Modern C++ adds the range-based for loop for iterating containers.
cards.summary: for, while, do-while, and the range-based for.

cards.term: What is a range-based for loop?
cards.definition: `for (auto x : v) { ... }` iterates over every element of a container or array without an index. Use `for (auto& x : v)` to modify elements in place, and `for (const auto& x : v)` to read large elements without copying.
cards.summary: `for (const auto& x : v)` iterates a container directly.
cards.note: Use & to modify or to avoid copying; const& to read cheaply.

cards.term: In a `switch`, execution stops automatically at the end of each `case`.
cards.summary: False — without `break`, control falls through to the next case.
cards.definition: False. A `case` falls through into the following one unless you write `break` (or `return`). This fall-through is occasionally useful but is a classic source of bugs, so always `break` unless you mean to fall through.

cards.term: What is the difference between a declaration and a definition of a function?
cards.definition: A declaration (prototype) states the name, return type and parameter types and ends with `;` — e.g. `int add(int, int);`. It lets callers compile. A definition additionally provides the body `{ ... }`. A function may be declared many times but defined exactly once (the One Definition Rule).
cards.summary: Declaration = signature only; definition = signature + body.

cards.sub: A) by value copies the argument; by reference (`T&`) aliases the caller's object so changes are visible    B) they behave identically    C) by value lets the function modify the caller's variable    D) by reference always copies the data
cards.definition: Correct: A) by value copies the argument; by reference (`T&`) aliases the caller's object so changes are visible. Pass by value for small, cheap types when you want a private copy; pass by reference to mutate the caller's object or to avoid copying large ones.

cards.term: Why pass a large object as `const T&`?
cards.definition: `const T&` binds a reference to the caller's object (no copy of a potentially large `std::string` or `std::vector`) while `const` promises the function will not modify it. It is the default way to pass read-only parameters that are not cheap to copy.
cards.summary: Avoids a copy (reference) and guarantees read-only (const).

cards.definition: A parameter value supplied automatically when the caller omits it: `int pow(int b, int e = 2);` lets you call `pow(5)` (meaning `pow(5, 2)`) or `pow(5, 3)`. Defaults must be the trailing parameters and are given once, in the declaration.
cards.summary: A fallback value used when the caller omits that argument.

cards.definition: Defining several functions with the same name but different parameter lists; the compiler picks the match from the argument types. `print(int)` and `print(double)` are distinct overloads. The return type alone cannot distinguish overloads.
cards.summary: Same name, different parameter lists; resolved by arguments.

cards.term: A function whose return type is {{void}} returns no value and is called purely for its side effects.
cards.definition: `void log(const std::string& m);` performs an action (printing, mutating) but produces no result. A bare `return;` exits it early; you cannot return a value from it.

cards.term: Returning a reference or pointer to a local variable is a bug.
cards.summary: True — the local is destroyed when the function returns, leaving a dangling reference.
cards.definition: True. A local object's lifetime ends when the function returns, so a reference or pointer to it dangles and using it is undefined behaviour. Return by value instead, or return something whose lifetime outlives the call.

The region of code where a name is visible. A variable declared inside `{ ... }` is local to that block and destroyed at the closing brace; a name at file scope is global. Inner scopes can shadow outer names of the same identifier.
cards.summary: The block in which a name is visible and alive.

cards.term: What is recursion, and what does a recursive function need?
cards.definition: A function that calls itself. It needs a base case that stops the recursion and a recursive step that moves toward it. `factorial(0)=1` (base), `factorial(n)=n*factorial(n-1)` (step). Missing or unreachable base cases cause infinite recursion and stack overflow.
cards.summary: Self-calling function; needs a base case + a step toward it.

cards.term: What does `break` vs `continue` do inside a loop?
cards.sub: A) `break` exits the loop entirely; `continue` skips to the next iteration    B) both exit the loop    C) `continue` exits the loop; `break` skips an iteration    D) both skip one iteration
cards.definition: Correct: A) `break` exits the loop entirely; `continue` skips to the next iteration. `break` jumps past the end of the nearest enclosing loop or switch; `continue` abandons the rest of the current iteration and re-evaluates the loop condition.

cards.term: The ternary conditional operator writes a small if/else inline: `int m = (a > b) {{?}} a : b;` picks the larger value.
cards.summary: cond ? whenTrue : whenFalse — an expression form of if/else.
cards.definition: `cond ? x : y` evaluates to `x` if `cond` is true, otherwise `y`. It is an expression, so it can appear inside an initialiser or argument, unlike a statement-level `if`.

cards.term: Exercise: write a function that returns whether an integer is even, taking the argument the idiomatic way.
cards.sub: Think value vs reference for a cheap type, and const-correctness.
cards.definition: `bool is_even(int n) { return n % 2 == 0; }`. An `int` is small and cheap, so pass by value; the function does not modify its argument and returns a fresh `bool`, so no reference is needed. (Passing `const int&` would work but is needless overhead for a fundamental type.)
cards.summary: `bool is_even(int n){ return n%2==0; }` — by value for a cheap type.

cards.term: Exercise: this loop never ends — why? `for (unsigned i = 5; i >= 0; --i) { ... }`
cards.sub: Consider what an unsigned value does when it goes below 0.
cards.definition: An `unsigned` can never be negative: when `i` is `0` and you do `--i`, it wraps around to the maximum unsigned value, so `i >= 0` is always true. Use a signed counter, or restructure the test (e.g. count up, or `while (i-- > 0)` with care).
cards.summary: Unsigned wraps below 0, so `i >= 0` is always true.
